2.blurb: Hongshan, in what is now northeastern China around Liaoning, is the earliest place on earth where people built hilltop stone altars, a goddess-temple, and ritual pits stuffed with exquisitely carved jade pig-dragons and coiled cloud-dragons — all around 3500 BCE, long before any Chinese dynasty existed. It's the first Chinese culture to act like it has full-blown organized religion.

While Eridu was stacking its first mud temple around 5000 BCE, the farmers of Mehrgarh in what is now Pakistan had already been growing wheat and barley, herding cattle, making cotton thread, and smelting copper for almost two thousand years. The Indus story starts earlier than Mesopotamia's first real cities — the Harappans were running their own deep Neolithic civilization in parallel, just with no writing to leave us a receipt.

3.blurb: Roughly twelve centuries after Uruk, the Indus Valley produced something just as astonishing: Mohenjo-daro and Harappa, planned brick cities laid out on a grid, with standardized bricks, covered drains, public bathhouses, and private toilets in individual houses. No palaces, no royal tombs, no obvious kings — a very different kind of urban revolution from the temple-run city-states of Sumer.

Erlitou, on the Yi River in central China around 1900–1550 BCE, is the oldest site in China big enough and organized enough to call a proper city — palaces on rammed-earth platforms, bronze workshops casting ritual vessels, and a planned center whose layout maps almost perfectly onto the legendary Xia dynasty the Chinese texts insisted was real. It's where Chinese legend finally meets archaeology.

4.matchText: Harappan burials from the same period
4.blurb: At Harappa and Mohenjo-daro, the graves look almost embarrassingly modest next to Ur's gold-drenched death pits: a pot or two, some shell bangles, a copper mirror, and the occasional bead necklace, spread evenly across rich and poor. No ruler was buried with stacks of treasure, no attendants were killed to follow anyone into the afterlife — whoever ran the Indus cities, they seem to have done it without making a show of it in death.

The Harappan goods that showed up at Dilmun came down a fully organized shipping lane. Indus sailors ran routes across 1,550 miles (2,500 km) of open ocean on the monsoon winds, stopping at Dilmun to transship carnelian, cotton, ivory, and hardwoods for onward sale to Sumer. The Dilmun entrepôt used both Mesopotamian shekels and Harappan cubical weights side by side because its merchants were dealing with both ends every day — one of the world's first international trading systems, running for over four centuries.

4.matchText: carnelian beads, etched stone weights, distinctive shell ornaments
4.blurb: These are the signature Harappan luxury exports. The long etched carnelian beads — some up to 5 inches (12 cm) — were made by heating the raw stone to turn it deep red and then drilling it end-to-end with copper drills tipped with abrasive paste, a weeks-long process whose precision wouldn't be matched anywhere in the ancient world. The beads in Queen Puabi's grave were imported at enormous expense from workshops 1,800 miles away on the Indus plain.

Shimao, a massive stone-walled town on the loess plateau around 2000 BCE, is one of the biggest and strangest late-Neolithic sites in China — and the builders buried dozens of sacrificed young women in pits beneath the city walls as foundation offerings, their decapitated heads arranged in neat rows. It's the same grim logic as Ur's death pits, on the opposite end of Eurasia, at roughly the same moment.

The 74 sacrificed attendants in Queen Puabi's death pit at Ur are the best-known Bronze Age example of royal retainer sacrifice — and they are an order of magnitude smaller than what was happening at the same time far upriver on the Nile. At Kerma in Upper Nubia, the largest royal tumuli contained three to four hundred sacrificed retainers arranged in corridors radiating out from the central burial chamber. The Indus Valley, alone among the four Bronze Age cradles, did not practice retainer sacrifice at all.

The lapis lazuli, carnelian, and other exotic stones piled into Queen Puabi's grave didn't grow in Mesopotamia. They were imported from Afghanistan and the Indus Valley via two long-distance trade pipelines: a maritime route through Harappan ships and a parallel overland route through the Elamite caravan network based at Susa and Anshan. Elam's wealth throughout the Bronze Age was built on being the Iranian middleman for these routes, and the Royal Tombs are physical evidence of what flowed through.

5.blurb: By Naram-Sin's time around 2250 BCE, Sargon's inscriptions had already been bragging for a generation about ships from Meluhha docking at Akkad. Whether Naram-Sin himself ever actually reached the Indus with an army is doubtful — no corroborating evidence on the Harappan side — but the sea route was real, constant, and running carnelian, cotton, and ivory west in exchange for Mesopotamian silver and textiles heading east. The Mesopotamians knew exactly who was on the other end of the pipeline.

The great Indus cities didn't burn and didn't get conquered — they just quietly emptied out between about 1900 and 1300 BCE. The Sarasvati river system dried up, the monsoon weakened, trade with Mesopotamia unraveled, and the Harappans scattered into smaller villages eastward. No single catastrophe, just a civilization slowly running out of the water and the connections that had made it possible.

Liangzhu, in the wet rice country south of the Yangtze, was pulling off something the Yellow River wouldn't manage for another thousand years: a full-scale state, with a jade-wearing elite, enormous earthen dams holding back seasonal floods, and a carefully planned ceremonial center. Then, around 2300 BCE, the whole system drowned — the dams failed, the center was abandoned, and China's first experiment with complex statehood ended underwater.

The gold Gudea was importing came out of the Wadi Allaqi valley east of the Nile in Lower Nubia — the same gold-bearing rock formations that would later bankroll the New Kingdom Egyptian treasury and fill the Kassite kings' requests in the Amarna Letters. The Nubian gold pipeline ran from Sumerian Lagash all the way through to Kassite Babylon, a millennium and a half of continuous extraction.

Sargon's Elamite campaign and the one his grandson Naram-Sin would later launch both produced a remarkable diplomatic follow-up: the Elamite-Akkadian Treaty of around 2250 BCE, preserved on a clay tablet in the Louvre. The treaty is the oldest surviving full political treaty in any language from any civilization, and it shows that in just one generation the Akkadian-Elamite relationship had shifted from Sargon's conquest-and-loot pattern to a bilateral mutual-defense agreement between two kings who considered themselves peers. Elam was never a subject province for long.

The reason you can see the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin in the Louvre today is that in 1158 BCE the Elamite king Shutruk-Nahhunte sacked Babylon, carried the stele off to Susa as a war trophy, and installed it in an Elamite temple. It sat there for nearly 3,000 years until French archaeologists excavated it in 1898. Without Shutruk-Nahhunte's looting, the stele would almost certainly have been destroyed or recycled by a later Mesopotamian campaign, and the single most famous piece of Akkadian royal art would not exist today.

6.blurb: The Harappa–Dilmun–Mesopotamia triangle ran for over four centuries on a steady flow of Indus carnelian, cotton, ivory, and tropical hardwoods going west in exchange for Mesopotamian silver, grain, and textiles coming east. When Ur III collapsed, the demand side of the pipeline began to wither at roughly the same moment the Harappan cities themselves started thinning out — two ends of a single commercial system going quiet within a single generation.

The Indus cities collapsed on almost the exact same timetable as Ur III. Mohenjo-daro's population thinned out, Harappa's bathhouse and granaries were abandoned, the standardized weights and seals stopped being made, and the long-distance trade with Meluhha that had filled Ur's royal tombs with carnelian beads simply dried up. Two of the three great Bronze Age cradles went quiet within a single generation.

The Elamite king who led the attack was Kindattu of the Shimashki dynasty, the highland-based royal house that had taken over in Susa a generation earlier. Kindattu didn't intend to erase Sumer when he sacked Ur — he intended to loot a rival and go home. But the accidental consequence of the raid was the end of Sumerian political history. The Amorites flowed into the vacated plain, and the surviving Sumerian cities became increasingly Akkadian-speaking. Ur III's collapse was the event that broke Sumer as a polity, and an Elamite king pulled the trigger.

Ibbi-Sin was carried specifically to Anshan — the highland Elamite capital, not the lowland city of Susa. The detail matters because it tells us where Elamite royal authority was actually centered at that moment: in the mountain stronghold of Fars, not in the plain city that most later sources associate with Elam. The Shimashki royal line was highland-based, and the deposed Ur III king lived out his captivity somewhere in the Zagros valleys far above the Mesopotamian plain he had ruled.

8.blurb: After the Harappan cities emptied out, a new culture arrives on the Indian subcontinent around 1500 BCE: horse-driving, chariot-riding Indo-European speakers composing the hymns that became the Rigveda, praising gods named Indra, Mitra, and Varuna — the same gods Mitanni's ruling class was invoking in treaties with Egypt at the same moment. The Vedic world and the Near Eastern great-power club were two ends of the same cultural thread.

The gold the Kassite kings and their fellow Great Kings were begging the pharaoh for in the Amarna Letters was specifically Nubian gold. Egypt's colonial province in Nubia, run by the Viceroy of Kush out of the administrative town of Aniba, was producing gold on an industrial scale from the Wadi Allaqi mines, and the pharaoh's ability to say yes or no to his fellow kings depended entirely on the flow out of those mines. The Amarna Letters are the diplomatic other end of Nubia's gold pipeline.

Elam Chapter 6 tells the sack of Babylon from the other side — the Middle Elamite Shutrukid dynasty at its peak, the sixty-day siege and systematic temple-by-temple looting, the Kassite princess in the Elamite royal ancestry as the rhetorical excuse, and the route the trophies took over the Zagros passes to Susa, where French archaeologists recovered most of them from the rubble in 1901.

Elam was the fifth member of the Late Bronze Age Great Power club that corresponded through the Amarna Letters — alongside New Kingdom Egypt, the Hittites, Kassite Babylonia, and Mitanni. Its ruling Igihalkid dynasty was mid-campaign on the building program that would produce Untash-Napirisha's Chogha Zanbil ziggurat and the bronze statue of Queen Napir-Asu, the largest solid-cast bronze of the ancient Near East. Elam of the Amarna era was a peer power, not a frontier kingdom.

13.blurb: While Mesopotamia was inventing writing, law, and empire between 5000 and 1550 BCE, China was running its own completely independent experiment: millet and rice domesticated in two separate centers, Yangshao painted pottery, Hongshan jade, the drowned Liangzhu state, Longshan walled towns, the Yu the Great flood legend, and finally Erlitou — the first Chinese city big enough to match the legends of the Xia dynasty. Different script, different cities, different gods, and no Mesopotamian input anywhere along the way.

Cyrus's own Cyrus Cylinder identifies him as 'king of Anshan, son of Cambyses king of Anshan, grandson of Cyrus king of Anshan, great-grandson of Teispes king of Anshan, of an enduring royal line.' Anshan was the highland capital of Elam for almost three thousand years before Cyrus was born. The founder of the Persian Empire is, in his own royal titulature, the heir of the Elamite kingdom that Ashurbanipal had destroyed a century earlier. The Persian Empire is, genealogically, a continuation of Elam — not a Persian takeover of Mesopotamia but an Elamite restoration wearing Persian clothes.

The primary bureaucratic language of the Persian Empire for the first century was not Aramaic. It was Elamite. The Persepolis Fortification Archive — 30,000 clay tablets recording the daily operations of the imperial court between 509 and 494 BCE — is written in Elamite cuneiform by scribes whose training went back to the old Susa schools. The Behistun Inscription's middle version is also in Elamite, and was carved first. The Achaemenid state ran on Elamite language and Elamite scribes until the bureaucratic shift to Aramaic in the late fifth century BCE.

7.blurb: Donkey caravans were the Bronze Age's standard solution for bulk overland trade. Five hundred years before the Assyrian merchants were hauling tin and textiles up to Kanesh, the Egyptian official Harkhuf was leading caravans of three hundred donkeys south into Nubia for ebony, ivory, incense, and exotic goods — one of the earliest and best-documented donkey-caravan operations in human history.

The Mari diplomatic archives show a different Hammurabi from the master strategist of this chapter. For the first fifteen years of his reign, Hammurabi addressed the Elamite sukkalmah Siwe-palar-hupak using the respect-ranked kinship term 'father' — the diplomatic language of a junior power acknowledging a senior one. Elam was the great power at the start of Hammurabi's career, and Babylon ranked below it. Only after Siwe-palar-hupak overextended himself in the 1760s BCE did Hammurabi switch sides and begin building the Babylonian supremacy this chapter describes.

7.matchText: in 1158 BCE it was looted from the Babylonian temple-city of
7.blurb: The actual date is 1158 BCE, and the Elamite king who took it was Shutruk-Nahhunte. He didn't take it from Babylon itself — the Code stele had been installed at the religious city of Sippar in northern Babylonia, and that's where the Elamite army found it during the great sack of 1158. Shutruk-Nahhunte erected it in the temple of Inshushinak at Susa, where it remained until Jacques de Morgan excavated it in 1901.

11.blurb: Taharqa was a Kushite pharaoh of Egypt's 25th Dynasty — a descendant of the Egyptianized Nubian elite who inherited Egypt's former Nubian province after the Bronze Age collapse and, three centuries later, marched north and conquered Egypt itself. Ancient Nubia Ch 8 tells the setup: how the bicultural Nubian nobility absorbed the cult of Amun at Jebel Barkal and carried it forward when Egypt's state apparatus withdrew around 1070 BCE.

11.matchText: Ashurbanipal then decided to deal with
11.blurb: Ashurbanipal's Elamite campaigns centered on the Battle of Til-Tuba in 653 BCE, where the Assyrian army killed the Elamite king Te-Umman, decapitated him on the battlefield, and brought his head back to Nineveh — where Ashurbanipal had himself depicted reclining in his palace garden drinking wine while the severed head dangled from a tree above him. The famous Garden Party Relief in the British Museum is the image that came out of that campaign, and it is one of the most famous pieces of ancient Near Eastern propaganda art.

The 'destruction of Elam' language is true of the kingdom but not of the civilization. The Elamite scribal tradition survived the sack of Susa by moving upland to Persian Anshan, where it was absorbed into the administration of the new Persian royal line that would produce Cyrus the Great 140 years later. The Persepolis Fortification Archive (30,000 clay tablets recording Persian imperial administration from 509–494 BCE) is in Elamite, not Persian. Elam didn't fade after Ashurbanipal — it became the bureaucratic spine of the empire that succeeded Assyria.

9.blurb: The Elamite king Nebuchadnezzar I defeated was Hutelutush-Inshushinak, the last king of the Shutrukid dynasty. Nebuchadnezzar sacked Susa, recovered the Marduk statue that Shutruk-Nahhunte had carried off fifty years earlier — but crucially left behind the other trophies, including the Code of Hammurabi and the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin. That's why those monuments were still at Susa for French archaeologists to find in 1898–1901. Nebuchadnezzar got Marduk back. He did not get Hammurabi's law code back, and the world is better off for his oversight.
